ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for keema curry; 'curry (food product)' is the closest defensible parent class. Keema curry is a minced-meat curry dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (meat-based curry dish) used as closest defensible match; no specific keema curry code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of keema curry in Singapore is approximately 150g, served as a side dish alongside rice or flatbread such as roti prata.

nutrient_source.title: Keema Curry (Minced Mutton, Potato, Cauliflower, Onion, Coconut Milk, Curry Powder)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for keema curry as described: minced mutton, potato, cauliflower, onion, fried and cooked in coconut milk and curry powder. Confidence 0.95 as reported in source record. Saturated fat estimated at 5.5g/100g based on typical mutton and coconut milk composition per HPB/USDA cross-reference, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and micronutrient detail not reported by HPB for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for keema curry. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous meat-and-vegetable curry dishes with coconut milk from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The dish is dominated by minced mutton (protein and fat, negligible GI contribution), low-starch vegetables (cauliflower, onion), and a small amount of potato; coconut milk contributes fat which further blunts glycaemic response. Carbohydrate content is only 2.69g/100g, resulting in a very low glycaemic load. GL per serving = (35 × 2.69 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 369mg/100g in the borderline 200–400mg range; saturated fat ~5.5g/100g contributes to arterial stiffness; potassium ~280mg/100g and dietary fibre 1.86g/100g provide partial but insufficient offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100g or less, request reduced-salt preparation, and pair with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach or peas to improve the sodium-potassium balance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.69g/100g) and minimal sugar (0.43g/100g) result in negligible glycaemic impact; high protein (12g/100g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability; estimated GI of 35 is low.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Keema curry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; lean minced meat variants are preferred to limit saturated fat, which can worsen insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at ~5.5g/100g from mutton and coconut milk approaches the threshold for cardiovascular concern; sodium at 369mg/100g adds to cumulative daily sodium load.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size, choose lean minced chicken or turkey as a substitute for mutton, and use reduced-fat coconut milk to lower saturated fat and support heart health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced red meat (mutton) is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying pro-inflammatory pathways; saturated fat ~5.5g/100g is at the RA threshold; anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) are present but insufficient to offset the red meat burden.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute minced mutton with minced chicken, turkey, or lentils to retain the anti-inflammatory spice benefits while eliminating the arachidonic acid load from red meat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keema curry is a spiced minced-meat curry dish popular in Singapore's Mamak (Tamil Muslim) culinary tradition, as well as across South Asia. The Singapore version typically uses minced mutton cooked with diced potato, cauliflower florets, and onion, fried and then simmered in coconut milk with curry powder and a blend of aromatic spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, and garlic. The result is a richly flavoured, moderately thick curry with a savoury, mildly spiced profile and a creamy coconut undertone. The texture is soft and moist, with the minced meat providing a fine-grained body and the vegetables adding substance. Nutritionally, keema curry is high in protein (12g/100g) and fat (12.13g/100g), with very low carbohydrate (2.69g/100g) and minimal sugar (0.43g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Sodium is 369mg/100g, placing it in the borderline range for hypertension management. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 5.5g/100g from the mutton and coconut milk base. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside roti prata, rice, or bread in Mamak restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore. The anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provides phytochemical benefits, though the red meat base is a consideration for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular risk.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the source record. The dish description matches the HPB entry exactly. Saturated fat is estimated from mutton and coconut milk composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework given the very low carbohydrate content of the dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific keema curry preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central does not contain an exact match for this Singaporean Mamak-style keema curry. Potassium estimated at 280mg/100g based on typical mutton and vegetable composition per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-reference; this value is approximate. Saturated fat estimated at 5.5g/100g based on typical mutton (~3.5g/100g) and coconut milk (~2g/100g contribution at typical recipe proportions) per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The input dietary_tags included 'plant_based' which is inconsistent with a minced mutton dish and has been removed. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for keema curry was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 2.69 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.4.
